title: My employer did not pay me for 2 months, what should I do?

messages.content: My employer did not pay me for 2 months, what should I do?

triage_result.Original Input: My employer did not pay me for 2 months, what should I do?
triage_result.Normalized English: The user is seeking legal guidance regarding the non-payment of wages by their employer for a period of two months.

metadata.title: . Time of payment. Wages shall be paid at least once every two (2) weeks or twice a month at
metadata.parent_context:
. Time of payment. Wages shall be paid at least once every two (2) weeks or twice a month at 
intervals not exceeding sixteen (16) days. If on account of force majeure or circumstances beyond the 
employer’s control, payment of wages on or within the time herein provided cannot be made, the employer 
shall pay the wages immediately after such force majeure or circumstances have ceased. No employer shall 
make payment with less frequency than once a month. 
The payment of wages of employees engaged to perform a task which cannot be completed in two (2) weeks 
shall be subject to the following conditions, in the absence of a collective bargaining agreement or arbitration 
award: 
1. That payments are made at intervals not exceeding sixteen (16) days, in proportion to the amount of 
work completed; 
   
2. That final settlement is made upon completion of the work.

rag_chunks.text:
. Time of payment. Wages shall be paid at least once every two (2) weeks or twice a month at 
intervals not exceeding sixteen (16) days. If on account of force majeure or circumstances beyond the 
employer’s control, payment of wages on or within the time herein provided cannot be made, the employer 
shall pay the wages immediately after such force majeure or circumstances have ceased. No employer shall 
make payment with less frequency than once a month. 
The payment of wages of employees engaged to perform a task which cannot be completed in two (2) weeks 
shall be subject to the following conditions, in the absence of a collective bargaining agreement or arbitration 
award: 
1. That payments are made at intervals not exceeding sixteen (16) days, in proportion to the amount of 
work completed; 
   
2. That final settlement is made upon completion of the work.
rag_chunks.excerpt:
. Time of payment. Wages shall be paid at least once every two (2) weeks or twice a month at 
intervals not exceeding sixteen (16) days. If on account of force majeure or circumstances beyond the

metadata.title: Deemed termination of contract under section 7
metadata.source_file: Cap 57 Consolidated version for the Whole Chapter (24-08-2025) (English).pdf
metadata.parent_context:
(1)	
Without prejudice to the rights of an employee under common 
law, an employee may terminate his contract of employment 
without notice or payment in lieu of notice if any wages 
are not paid within one month from the day on which they 
become due to him under section 23.
	
(2)	
Where a contract of employment is terminated under 
subsection (1), the contract shall be deemed to be terminated 
by the employer in accordance with section 7 and the 
employer shall be deemed to have agreed to pay to the 
employee the sum specified in section 7.
(Added 74 of 1997 s. 5)

rag_chunks.text:
(1)	
Without prejudice to the rights of an employee under common 
law, an employee may terminate his contract of employment 
without notice or payment in lieu of notice if any wages 
are not paid within one month from the day on which they 
become due to him under section 23.
	
(2)	
Where a contract of employment is terminated under 
subsection (1), the contract shall be deemed to be terminated 
by the employer in accordance with section 7 and the 
employer shall be deemed to have agreed to pay to the 
employee the sum specified in section 7.
(Added 74 of 1997 s. 5)

metadata.title: Calculation of average monthly wages for section 32NA
metadata.source_file: Cap 57 Consolidated version for the Whole Chapter (24-08-2025) (English).pdf
metadata.parent_context:
(1)	
This section applies in calculating an employee’s average 
monthly wages for the purposes of section 32NA(1)(b)(ii).
	
(2)	
In subsections (3), (4) and (5)—
wages (工資) includes a sum of money paid by an employer in 
respect of any of the following days—
	
(a)	
a day of maternity leave, a day of paternity leave, a rest 
day, a sickness day, a holiday or a day of annual leave 
taken by the employee;
	
(b)	
a day of leave taken by the employee with the agreement 
of the employer;
	
(c)	
a normal working day on which the employee is not 
provided with work by the employer;
	
(d)	
a day of absence from work of the employee due to 
temporary incapacity for which compensation is payable 
under section 10 of the Employees’ Compensation 
Ordinance (Cap. 282).
	
(3)	
The employee’s average monthly wages are the average 
monthly wages earned by the employee during—
	
(a)	
the period of 12 months immediately before the date of 
termination of the contract of employment; or
	
(b)	
if the employee has been employed by the employer for 
a period shorter than 12 months immediately before the 
date of termination of the contract of employment—the 
shorter period.
Last updated date
16.6.2023
Employment Ordinance
Part VIA	
6A-40
Section 32NB	
Cap. 57
Verified Copy
	
(4)	
The average monthly wages are to be calculated without 
regard to—
	
(a)	
any period (excluded period) during the 12-month 
period or shorter period for which the employee was not 
paid wages or full wages because of—
	
(i)	
any maternity leave, paternity leave, rest day, 
sickness day, holiday or annual leave taken by the 
employee;
	
(ii)	
any leave taken by the employee with the 
agreement of the employer;
	
(iii)	
the employee’s not being provided with work by 
the employer on a normal working day; or
	
(iv)	
the employee’s absence from work due to 
temporary incapacity for which compensation 
is payable under section 10 of the Employees’ 
Compensation Ordinance (Cap. 282); and
	
(b)	
any wages paid to the employee for the excluded period.
	
(5)	
To avoid doubt, if the amount of the wages paid to an 
employee in respect of a day covered by the definition of 
wages in subsection (2) is only a fraction of the amount 
earned by the employee on a normal working day, the 
employee’s average monthly wages are to be calculated 
without regard to the wages and the day.
	
(6)	
Despite subsection (3), if for any reason it is impracticable to 
calculate an employee’s average monthly wages in the manner 
provided in that subsection, the amount may be calculated by 
reference to—
	
(a)	
the wages earned by a person who was employed at the 
same work by the same employer during the period of 
12 months immediately before the date of termination of 
the employee’s contract of employment; or
Last updated date
16.6.2023
Employment Ordinance
Part VIA	
6A-42
Section 32NB	
Cap. 57
Verified Copy
	
(b)	
if there is no such person—the wages earned by a person 
who was employed in the same trade or occupation and 
at the same work in the same district during the period 
of 12 months immediately before the date of termination 
of the employee’s contract of employment.
(Added 21 of 2018 s. 5)

metadata.title: . Standards/Criteria for minimum wage fixing. The regional minimum wages to be established by
metadata.parent_context:
. Standards/Criteria for minimum wage fixing. The regional minimum wages to be established by 
the Regional Board shall be as nearly adequate as is economically feasible to maintain the minimum 
standards of living necessary for the health, efficiency and general well-being of the employees within the 
framework of the national economic and social development program. In the determination of such regional 
minimum wages, the Regional Board shall, among other relevant factors, consider the following: 
a. The demand for living wages; 
   
b. Wage adjustment vis-à-vis the consumer price index; 
   
c. The cost of living and changes or increases therein; 
   
d. The needs of workers and their families; 
   
e. The need to induce industries to invest in the countryside; 
   
f. Improvements in standards of living; 
   
g. The prevailing wage levels; 
   
h. Fair return of the capital invested and capacity to pay of employers; 
   
i. Effects on employment generation and family income; and 
   
j. The equitable distribution of income and wealth along the imperatives of economic and social 
development.  
The wages prescribed in accordance with the provisions of this Title shall be the standard prevailing minimum 
wages in every region. These wages shall include wages varying with industries, provinces or localities if in 
the judgment of the Regional Board, conditions make such local differentiation proper and necessary to 
effectuate the purpose of this Title. 
Any person, company, corporation, partnership or any other entity engaged in business shall file and register 
annually with the appropriate Regional Board, Commission and the National Statistics Office, an itemized 
listing of their labor component, specifying the names of their workers and employees below the managerial 
level, including learners, apprentices and disabled/handicapped workers who were hired under the terms 
prescribed in the employment contracts, and their corresponding salaries and wages. 
22 
Downloaded from the Department of Labor and Employment, Republic of the Philippines - 29 September 2011  
http://www.dole.gov.ph/labor_codes.php?id=34 
 
 
Where the application of any prescribed wage increase by virtue of a law or wage order issued by any 
Regional Board results in distortions of the wage structure within an establishment, the employer and the 
union shall negotiate to correct the distortions. Any dispute arising from wage distortions shall be resolved 
through the grievance procedure under their collective bargaining agreement and, if it remains unresolved, 
through voluntary arbitration. Unless otherwise agreed by the parties in writing, such dispute shall be decided 
by the voluntary arbitrators within ten (10) calendar days from the time said dispute was referred to voluntary 
arbitration. 
In cases where there are no collective agreements or recognized labor unions, the employers and workers 
shall endeavor to correct such distortions. Any dispute arising therefrom shall be settled through the National 
Conciliation and Mediation Board and, if it remains unresolved after ten (10) calendar days of conciliation, 
shall be referred to the appropriate branch of the National Labor Relations Commission (NLRC). It shall be 
mandatory for the NLRC to conduct continuous hearings and decide the dispute within twenty (20) calendar 
days from the time said dispute is submitted for compulsory arbitration. 
The pendency of a dispute arising from a wage distortion shall not in any way delay the applicability of any 
increase in prescribed wage rates pursuant to the provisions of law or wage order. 
As used herein, a wage distortion shall mean a situation where an increase in prescribed wage rates results in 
the elimination or severe contraction of intentional quantitative differences in wage or salary rates between 
and among employee groups in an establishment as to effectively obliterate the distinctions embodied in such 
wage structure based on skills, length of service, or other logical bases of differentiation. 
All workers paid by result, including those who are paid on piecework, takay, pakyaw or task basis, shall 
receive not less than the prescribed wage rates per eight (8) hours of work a day, or a proportion thereof for 
working less than eight (8) hours. 
All recognized learnership and apprenticeship agreements shall be considered automatically modified insofar 
as their wage clauses are concerned to reflect the prescribed wage rates. (As amended by Republic Act No. 
6727, June 9, 1989)

metadata.title: Wage period
metadata.source_file: Cap 57 Consolidated version for the Whole Chapter (24-08-2025) (English).pdf
metadata.parent_context:
The wage period in respect of which wages are payable under 
a contract of employment shall, until the contrary is proved, be 
deemed to be 1 month.

rag_chunks.text:
The wage period in respect of which wages are payable under 
a contract of employment shall, until the contrary is proved, be 
deemed to be 1 month.

a contract of employment shall, until the contrary is proved, be

I understand this is a difficult situation. To provide a clear explanation, I need to clarify two key points so I can apply the correct general legal principles.

1.  Where are you working? (e.g., in Hong Kong or in the Philippines?)
2.  Are you still working for this employer, or has your employment ended?

Please provide a brief answer to these questions.
I am currently employed and residing here in Hong Kong, and my job is located here as well. i will quit this work once i receive my delayed salary

The user intends to resign upon receiving delayed salary. Under Hong Kong law, Section 32(1) states that an employee may terminate the contract without notice if wages are not paid within one month from the due date. Since wages are unpaid for two months, this condition is met. This means the user has the right to treat the contract as terminated by the employer, which is considered wrongful termination. This triggers entitlements under Section 7, which generally refers to severance or termination payments.

The context also mentions Section 32NB about calculating average monthly wages, which is relevant for determining any termination payments. The wage period is deemed to be one month unless proven otherwise.

The Philippine Labor Code provisions in the context are not applicable since the jurisdiction is Hong Kong. I should focus on explaining the Hong Kong law implications.

The user is still employed, so the situation involves ongoing unpaid wages and potential constructive dismissal. I need to explain the general principle: unpaid wages for over a month gives the employee the right to terminate without notice, and this termination is treated as employer-initiated, leading to potential claims for outstanding wages and other termination payments.

I should also provide relevant contacts for assistance in Hong Kong, like the MWO and Hong Kong Labour Department. Since the user is an OFW, the MWO (formerly POLO) is the primary agency for assistance. The Hong Kong Labour Department's Foreign Domestic Helper Hotline is also relevant if the user is a domestic worker, but I'll keep it general.

I'll structure the response: acknowledge the situation, explain the legal principle from Section 32, discuss what it generally means in practice (right to terminate, deemed dismissal, claiming wages and termination pay), and provide relevant contacts. Keep it simple and avoid legal jargon.

Thank you for that clarification. I understand you are working in Hong Kong and have not been paid for two months. This is a serious matter, and it's understandable you are considering resigning.

Based on the legal context provided, here is a general explanation of how Hong Kong labor law applies to your situation.

### General Legal Explanation

The core legal principle here comes from **Section 32 of Hong Kong's Employment Ordinance**. This section states that if an employer fails to pay wages *within one month* of the date they are due, the employee has the right to terminate their employment contract **immediately, without giving notice or making a payment in lieu of notice**.

*   **What this means for you:** Since your wages are two months overdue, you have already passed this one-month threshold. This means the law generally recognizes your right to resign immediately over this issue.

Furthermore, the law treats this situation in a specific way:
*   If you terminate your contract under this provision (for unpaid wages), the law **deems the contract to have been terminated by the employer**.
*   This is significant because a termination by the employer, rather than a voluntary resignation, typically triggers the employer's obligation to pay statutory termination benefits, such as severance pay or long service pay (if you are eligible), in addition to all your outstanding wages.

### What This Situation Generally Means in Practice

1.  **Outstanding Wages:** Your employer has a clear obligation to pay you all wages owed for the two months you have worked.
2.  **Termination of Contract:** You have a strong basis to end your employment immediately due to this serious breach of contract by your employer.
3.  **Potential Termination Payments:** Because the law may view this as a termination by the employer, you may be entitled to other payments upon termination, calculated based on your length of service and final wages. The context provided includes **Section 32NB**, which outlines how "average monthly wages" are calculated for such purposes.
4.  **Wage Period:** The context also states that the wage period is generally deemed to be one month, which supports the understanding that wages should be paid regularly and not withheld for such a long period.

### Relevant Contacts for Assistance in Hong Kong

Given the non-payment of wages, it is common for workers in your situation to seek assistance from the following agencies. They can provide guidance, mediation services, and help you understand the process for making a claim.

*   **Migrant Workers Office (MWO) Hong Kong** (formerly POLO): This is the primary Philippine government office in Hong Kong that assists OFWs with labor issues.
    *   **Location:** Rooms 1201-1206, 12/F, United Centre Building, 95 Queensway, Admiralty.
    *   **Contacts:** +852 5529 1880 / +852 2866 0640

*   **Hong Kong Labour Department:** The local government agency that enforces Hong Kong's employment laws.
    *   **General Enquiry Hotline:** +852 2717 1771
    *   **Foreign Domestic Helper Hotline:** +852 2157 9537 (Useful if you are a domestic worker)

It is generally advisable to keep records of your employment contract, any pay slips, and documentation of the unpaid period (such as notes or communications with your employer) when seeking assistance.
Can you summarize that in one paragraph?

<think>
*   User wants a summary of the previously provided legal information in a single paragraph.
    *   Context provided is the same legal text.
    *   Persona: Atty. Veritas AI (warm, supportive, clear, human-like).
    *   Constraint: Base response only on the provided context.

    *   Jurisdiction: Hong Kong.
    *   Relevant Law: Section 32 of the Employment Ordinance.
    *   Core Rule: Employee can terminate the contract without notice if wages aren't paid within one month of the due date.
    *   Legal Effect: If terminated under this rule, it's deemed that the *employer* terminated the contract, which entitles the worker to payments under section 7.
    *    Calculation: Section 32NB deals with calculating average monthly wages.
    *   Wage Period: Deemed to be 1 month.

    *   Combine the points: Right to terminate due to 2 months' unpaid wages $\rightarrow$ no notice needed $\rightarrow$ treated as termination by employer $\rightarrow$ entitled to unpaid wages and termination payments.

    *   Avoid robotic list styles.
    *   Stay empathetic but direct.
    *   Ensure it's a single paragraph as requested.
</think>

Since your wages in Hong Kong have been unpaid for two months, you are generally entitled under Section 32 of the Employment Ordinance to terminate your contract immediately without giving notice. Because this occurs after wages have been overdue for more than one month, the law treats your departure not as a voluntary resignation, but as if the employer terminated your employment; this means you may be eligible to claim both your full outstanding wages and the termination payments specified under the law. For further help with this process, you can reach out to the Migrant Workers Office (MWO) or the Hong Kong Labour Department.

messages.llm_response_preview: You're very welcome! If you need any more assistance, feel free to ask. Take care, and I hope your situation is resolved soon. 😊
